verses.text: Jehoahaz of Israel does evil, and God delivers him into the hand of Syria, which grinds Israel down year after year. Sin's consequence is real oppression. A people who will not have God as their refuge soon find no refuge from their enemies.

Jehoahaz of Israel does evil, and God delivers him into the hand of Syria, which grinds Israel down year after year. Sin's consequence is real oppression. A people who will not have God as their refuge soon find no refuge from their enemies.

Jehoahaz of Israel does evil, and God delivers him into the hand of Syria, which grinds Israel down year after year. Sin's consequence is real oppression. A people who will not have God as their refuge soon find no refuge from their enemies.

Yet when Jehoahaz pleads, God hears and gives Israel a saviour, easing Syria's grip — though they still keep the calves and the grove. Even in their unfaithfulness God shows mercy to a cry of distress. His patience outruns His people's repentance.

Yet when Jehoahaz pleads, God hears and gives Israel a saviour, easing Syria's grip — though they still keep the calves and the grove. Even in their unfaithfulness God shows mercy to a cry of distress. His patience outruns His people's repentance.

Yet when Jehoahaz pleads, God hears and gives Israel a saviour, easing Syria's grip — though they still keep the calves and the grove. Even in their unfaithfulness God shows mercy to a cry of distress. His patience outruns His people's repentance.

Israel is reduced to a tiny army, like dust at threshing, and Jehoahaz dies. The kingdom withers under judgment. What sin slowly drains away, no king can restore until the people truly return to the God they forsook.

Israel is reduced to a tiny army, like dust at threshing, and Jehoahaz dies. The kingdom withers under judgment. What sin slowly drains away, no king can restore until the people truly return to the God they forsook.

Israel is reduced to a tiny army, like dust at threshing, and Jehoahaz dies. The kingdom withers under judgment. What sin slowly drains away, no king can restore until the people truly return to the God they forsook.

Jehoash succeeds his father and likewise does evil, walking in Jeroboam's sins, yet he reigns and makes war. The northern throne passes from one idolater to the next. A dynasty unwilling to repent simply repeats itself, sin handed down like an heirloom.

Elisha lies dying, and king Jehoash weeps over him, 'the chariot of Israel.' The prophet has him take a bow and lay his hands on it, with the prophet's hands upon his. Even at death's door, God's servant points the king to trust the LORD for victory.

Elisha lies dying, and king Jehoash weeps over him, 'the chariot of Israel.' The prophet has him take a bow and lay his hands on it, with the prophet's hands upon his. Even at death's door, God's servant points the king to trust the LORD for victory.

Elisha lies dying, and king Jehoash weeps over him, 'the chariot of Israel.' The prophet has him take a bow and lay his hands on it, with the prophet's hands upon his. Even at death's door, God's servant points the king to trust the LORD for victory.

Elisha bids him shoot 'the arrow of the LORD's deliverance,' then strike the ground; the king strikes only thrice, and Elisha is angry, for he might have destroyed Syria utterly. Half-hearted faith receives a half-measure of blessing. God gives as boldly as we lay hold.

Elisha bids him shoot 'the arrow of the LORD's deliverance,' then strike the ground; the king strikes only thrice, and Elisha is angry, for he might have destroyed Syria utterly. Half-hearted faith receives a half-measure of blessing. God gives as boldly as we lay hold.

Elisha bids him shoot 'the arrow of the LORD's deliverance,' then strike the ground; the king strikes only thrice, and Elisha is angry, for he might have destroyed Syria utterly. Half-hearted faith receives a half-measure of blessing. God gives as boldly as we lay hold.

Elisha dies, and later a corpse cast hastily into his grave revives the moment it touches his bones. Life clings to the prophet even in death. God's power is not exhausted by the grave; the word and works of His servant outlast their burial.

Elisha dies, and later a corpse cast hastily into his grave revives the moment it touches his bones. Life clings to the prophet even in death. God's power is not exhausted by the grave; the word and works of His servant outlast their burial.

As Elisha foretold, Jehoash defeats Syria three times and recovers Israel's cities — for God was gracious for His covenant's sake, unwilling yet to destroy them. Mercy springs not from Israel's merit but from God's old promise to the fathers. His covenant holds when His people fail.
